name: Harlow Crossing
tagline: The Growth Side of the River

notes: Two-thirds of households have at least one commuter driving 40+ minutes each way
Built out in three waves: postwar ranches (1950s), Fairview (1990s), the cul-de-sac lots (2000s)
The Route 9 strip carries 14,000 vehicles a day — and every school bus in town

character: The growth side of the river: the Route 9 strip, the plaza, the school, the subdivisions with basketball hoops at the curb. Young families, long commutes, delivery cars and bus routes — every pothole on Route 9 is a line item in somebody's household budget. Harlow Crossing didn't work the mill; it grew up in its shadow after the machines went quiet.

landmarks.name: Harlow Elementary School
landmarks.description: 1968 brick school with the biggest gymnasium in town — which is why town meeting happens here

landmarks.name: Harlow Plaza
landmarks.description: The strip: pharmacy, bank branch, barber, laundromat — and the washboard pavement out front

landmarks.name: Rocco's Slice House
landmarks.description: Route 9 pizzeria since 1995; league photos on the wall, axle-repair receipts in the register drawer

landmarks.name: Harlow Congregational Church
landmarks.description: White steeple, 1841 — older than the mill; June Hagen does the flowers, and the choir forgives all voices

landmarks.name: Route 9
landmarks.description: The commercial strip and detour route: 14,000 cars a day, one famous frost heave, and Culvert 9 underneath

landmarks.name: Fairview Subdivision
landmarks.description: 1990s ranches and colonials; flags, hoops, and a back entrance that floods when Culvert 9 backs up

landmarks.name: Harlow Firehouse
landmarks.description: Volunteer Fire Company No. 2 — slab poured by volunteers in 1961; Engine 2 and the pancake breakfasts live here

landmarks.name: Community Fields
landmarks.description: Rec-league soccer and ball fields by the river bend; floods every April, beloved anyway
